stimulus:
The following text is adapted from Machado de Assis's 1881 novel The Posthumous Memoirs of Brás Cubas (translated from the Portuguese in 2020 by Flora Thomson-DeVeaux).

I may well be the most ordinary clerk who ever pushed a quill across a ledger, and, as my landlady used to mutter, the most distracted lodger any boarding house in Rio has had to suffer—at least before I came under his tutelage—but I did pass several seasons in the orbit of a singular old astronomer, a man with an unteachable instinct for the heavens. I sat beside his telescope through the cold of many evenings. I watched the patience with which he worked. I saw how a problem would settle into him like a stone into still water, then slowly raise the rings of an answer.
stem: Which choice best states the main idea of the text?

answerOptions.content: A truly insightful person prefers to develop ideas in silence before sharing them with others.
A speaker treasures the long stretch of time he spent in the company of a man he greatly admired.
A particularly gifted person feigns ordinariness so as not to overshadow those around him.
A person was lucky enough to win another's affection despite his evident shortcomings.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer because it most accurately states the main idea. The speaker describes himself as an unremarkable, distracted clerk but then turns to recall "several seasons" beside a "singular old astronomer" with "an unteachable instinct for the heavens." The phrases convey extended time and deep admiration.

Choice A is incorrect because the text does not describe how the astronomer expresses ideas to others; it depicts only his quiet method of work. Choice C is incorrect because the text presents the speaker, not the astronomer, as the humble figure and gives no sign the astronomer is hiding talent. Choice D is incorrect because the text concerns professional admiration, not affection or love.
